design_philosophy.core_loop: Choose objectives (trading, piracy, treasure hunting, family rescue, or combat), execute operations across Caribbean map, manage crew and ship resources, accumulate wealth and rank, adapt to dynamic geopolitical shifts
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Freedom versus consequence—player chooses playstyle but must adapt to randomized diplomatic relations, aging mechanics, and shifting economic conditions that create organic challenges without scripted difficulty spikes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Living the full pirate lifecycle: commanding vessels, engaging in fencing duels and naval combat, negotiating with colonial powers, building reputation, and retiring with prestige or infamy
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical open-endedness paired with consequence-driven emergent difficulty. No predetermined end, no failure states, only retirement based on accumulated legacy. Rejects linear progression in favor of dynamic systems that reward adaptability, exploration, and strategic long-term thinking. Player age deteriorates combat effectiveness, forcing tactical evolution. Multiple valid win conditions (wealth, rank, family recovery, trade mastery)

difficulty.lives_system: No lives system; player character ages and health deteriorates over time, eventually forcing retirement
difficulty.checkpointing: Game continues until player retirement; no traditional fail states or game-overs
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Extreme agency—player selects starting era (1560–1680), which dramatically alters geopolitical balance, available ships, and economic conditions. Difficulty emerges from player's own strategic choices and adaptation to unpredictable random events rather than tuned difficulty levels

progression.upgrade_system: Ship acquisition and crew recruitment; no traditional XP or equipment trees. Player gains rank with nations, marital status, accumulated wealth, and land holdings, which unlock social positions at retirement
progression.unlock_mechanism: Diplomatic standing unlocks access to ports, privateer commissions, and raid opportunities. Aging and health degrade combat capabilities naturally over time
replayability_hooks: Era selection (1560–1680) completely reshapes geopolitical landscape and ship availability
Dynamic diplomatic relations between four nations shift randomly and persist throughout playthrough
Multiple valid playstyle paths (pirate, privateer, trader, bounty hunter, treasure seeker)
Randomized city economies and population statistics encourage different trading strategies each game
Family member locations randomized, requiring different exploration strategies
Aging system guarantees no two runs have identical difficulty curves
Retirement scoring varies based on accumulated accomplishments, encouraging different objectives across playthroughs

world_building_techniques: Historical setting (16th–18th century Caribbean) with four colonial powers in dynamic conflict
Dynamic city statistics (wealth, population, fleet strength) reflect geopolitical shifts and tell emergent stories without cutscenes
Player's family members scattered across Caribbean as personal motivation for exploration
Era selection establishes historical context: 1560 shows Spain's dominance; 1680 shows mature multi-national competition
Privateer commissions and letters of marque contextualize player's actions within colonial politics

faction_indicators: Four playable nations: Spanish Empire, Dutch Republic, Kingdom of England, French colonial empire
Player can hold simultaneous rank with multiple nations or switch allegiances
Privateering commissions versus piracy represent ideological split
Marital status and spouse selection influence social standing
danger_signals: Deteriorating health forces character retirement, ending playstyle
Diplomatic shifts can instantly eliminate profitable opportunities
Aging reduces combat effectiveness, requiring strategic evolution mid-game
Crew recruitment becomes harder as player ages, limiting military options
environmental_storytelling: Colonial ports reflect national dominance in respective eras
Ship types are era and nation-specific, showing technological and imperial progression
City wealth and population fluctuate based on unrepresented offscreen economic/military events
Caribbean geography itself becomes strategic/narrative element (invisible barriers define playable world)
